Security and compliance — best practices for securing data and models in serverless ML pipelines
Auto-scaling and load balancing — strategies for dynamically scaling serverless workloads to handle changing traffic patterns

knowledge.what_it_is: Image processing fundamentals are essential skills for AI and data scientists to understand how images can be analyzed, manipulated, and understood by machines. This includes techniques such as image filtering, thresholding, edge detection, and feature extraction.
what_you_will_learn: Pixel representation — understanding how digital images are represented using pixels, color models (RGB, HSV, YUV), and bit depth
Image filtering — applying convolutional operations to blur, sharpen, or emboss images (Gaussian filter, median filter, Sobel operator)
Thresholding — segmenting images based on intensity values (binary thresholding, Otsu's method, adaptive thresholding)
Edge detection — identifying edges in images using techniques such as Canny edge detection, Sobel operator, and Laplacian of Gaussian
Feature extraction — extracting relevant features from images for classification or recognition tasks (SIFT, SURF, ORB)
Image segmentation — dividing an image into its constituent parts or objects (thresholding, clustering, active contour models)
Image enhancement — improving the quality of images using techniques such as contrast stretching, histogram equalization, and unsharp masking
Image compression — reducing the size of images while preserving their essential features (JPEG, PNG, GIF)
Image denoising — removing noise from images to improve their quality (Gaussian filter, median filter, non-local means algorithm)
Image registration — aligning multiple images of the same scene or object (feature-based methods, intensity-based methods)
Image feature descriptors — extracting and describing local features in images for recognition tasks (SIFT, SURF, BRISK)
Deep learning-based image processing — using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to perform tasks such as image classification, segmentation, and generation
Optical flow estimation — computing the motion of pixels between two images (Lucas-Kanade algorithm, Horn-Schunck method)
Image super-resolution — enhancing the resolution of low-quality images using techniques such as interpolation, convolutional neural networks, or deep learning-based methods

knowledge.what_it_is: Object detection is a fundamental task in computer vision that involves identifying and localizing objects within images or videos. YOLO (You Only Look Once) and R-CNN (Region-based Convolutional Neural Networks) are two popular architectures for object detection.
what_you_will_learn: YOLOv3 — the latest version of the YOLO architecture, achieving state-of-the-art performance on various benchmarks
R-CNN — a region proposal network that generates regions of interest (RoIs) to be classified by a CNN
Anchor boxes — a technique used in YOLO to predict object locations and sizes within predefined anchor boxes
Non-maximum suppression (NMS) — a post-processing step to eliminate duplicate detections and retain the most confident ones
Objectness score — a measure of how likely an object is present at a given location, used in R-CNN for region proposal
Region proposal network (RPN) — a CNN that generates proposals of potential objects within an image
Fast R-CNN — a faster version of the original R-CNN architecture, using a single forward pass through the network
Faster R-CNN — an even faster version of Fast R-CNN, using a GPU-accelerated region proposal network
YOLOv4 — an improved version of YOLOv3, with enhanced performance and accuracy on various object detection tasks
SSD (Single Shot Detector) — a real-time object detection architecture that uses a single neural network to predict object locations and classes
Mask R-CNN — an extension of Faster R-CNN that adds a segmentation branch to predict pixel masks for each detected object
Object detection metrics — understanding metrics such as precision, recall, F1-score, and mean average precision (mAP)
Data augmentation techniques — applying transformations to images to increase the diversity of the training dataset
Transfer learning — using pre-trained models as a starting point for fine-tuning on specific object detection tasks

knowledge.what_it_is: Image segmentation is a fundamental task in computer vision that involves partitioning an image into its constituent regions or objects, each with its own unique characteristics. It is a crucial step in many applications such as medical imaging, autonomous driving, and object recognition.
what_you_will_learn: U-Net architecture — a popular convolutional neural network (CNN) design for image segmentation tasks, consisting of an encoder-decoder structure with skip connections
FCN (Fully Convolutional Network) — a CNN architecture that replaces fully connected layers with convolutional layers to produce dense pixel-wise predictions
DeepLabv3+ — a state-of-the-art image segmentation model that uses atrous spatial pyramid pooling (ASPP) and encoder-decoder structure for accurate object detection
Mask R-CNN — an extension of Faster R-CNN that adds a branch for predicting object masks, enabling instance-level segmentation
Panoptic Segmentation — a task that combines semantic segmentation and instance segmentation to predict both the class label and instance mask for each pixel
Image-to-Image Translation (I2IT) — a technique used in image segmentation tasks to generate high-quality segmentations by translating input images into desired outputs
Adversarial Training — a method used to improve the robustness of image segmentation models against adversarial attacks by training on perturbed inputs
Attention Mechanisms — a technique used in image segmentation models to focus on relevant regions of the input image and ignore irrelevant ones
Multi-Task Learning (MTL) — a framework that enables training multiple tasks simultaneously, such as object detection and image segmentation, to improve overall performance
Transfer Learning — a technique used to leverage pre-trained models for image segmentation tasks by fine-tuning their weights on the target dataset
Data Augmentation Techniques — methods used to artificially increase the size of the training dataset, such as rotation, flipping, and color jittering, to improve model generalization
Loss Functions (Dice Loss, IoU Loss) — metrics used to evaluate the performance of image segmentation models, including Dice coefficient and Intersection over Union (IoU)
Post-processing Techniques (Connected Component Labeling, Watershed Transform) — methods used to refine the output segmentations by removing noise and improving boundaries
Object Proposal Networks (OPNs) — a technique used in image segmentation tasks to generate object proposals that can be refined into final segmentations

knowledge.what_it_is: Face recognition is a computer vision technique that uses machine learning algorithms to identify individuals based on their facial features, often used in applications such as security systems, surveillance, and access control.
what_you_will_learn: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) — designing CNN architectures for face recognition tasks, including LeNet, AlexNet, VGGFace
Facenet — a deep neural network architecture specifically designed for face verification and identification
Siamese Network — a type of neural network that learns to compare two input images and outputs a similarity metric
Triplet Loss — a loss function used in face recognition tasks to learn the relationship between anchor, positive, and negative faces
Face Embeddings — extracting features from facial images using CNNs or other techniques, such as PCA or LDA
Face Alignment — detecting and aligning facial landmarks to normalize face images for recognition
OpenCV's Face Detection API — using pre-trained models like Haar cascades or deep learning-based detectors
Dlib's Face Recognition API — using pre-trained models like the ResNet-34 architecture
TensorFlow's Face Recognition API — using pre-trained models like the InceptionResNetV2 architecture
Face Verification vs. Face Identification — understanding the differences between these two related tasks
Deep Learning-based Face Recognition Systems — designing and implementing systems that use deep learning techniques for face recognition

knowledge.what_it_is: OpenCV (Open Source Computer Vision Library) is a widely used, open-source library for computer vision tasks, including image and video processing, feature detection, object recognition, and more. It provides a comprehensive set of tools and functions for building applications that can interpret and understand visual data from images and videos.
what_you_will_learn: Image loading and saving — reading and writing image files in various formats (e.g., JPEG, PNG, BMP) using imread() and imwrite()
Image processing operations — applying filters, blurring, thresholding, and edge detection to images using functions like cv2.filter2D(), cv2.blur(), cv2.threshold(), and cv2.Canny()
Feature detection and description — detecting and describing features in images using functions like cv2.goodFeaturesToTrack(), cv2.featureDetector_create(), and cv2.BRISK.create()
Object recognition — recognizing objects in images using techniques like template matching, feature-based recognition, and deep learning-based methods (e.g., CNNs)
Image segmentation — segmenting images into regions of interest using functions like cv2.threshold(), cv2.findContours(), and cv2.watershed()
Face detection and recognition — detecting and recognizing faces in images using pre-trained Haar cascades, LBP, or deep learning-based methods (e.g., FaceNet)
Optical flow estimation — estimating the motion of pixels between two consecutive frames in a video sequence using functions like cv2.calcOpticalFlowPyrLK() and cv2.calcOpticalFlowFarneback()
Stereo matching — reconstructing 3D scenes from stereo pairs using functions like cv2.StereoBM_create(), cv2.StereoSGBM_create(), and cv2.reconstructScene()
Deep learning-based computer vision — applying CNNs, RNNs, or other deep learning architectures to computer vision tasks (e.g., image classification, object detection)
OpenCV's Python API — using OpenCV's Python bindings to access its functionality from Python scripts
OpenCV's C++ API — using OpenCV's C++ interface to access its functionality from C++ programs

knowledge.what_it_is: Text preprocessing is a crucial step in natural language processing (NLP) that involves cleaning, transforming, and normalizing text data to prepare it for analysis or modeling. In AI and Data Science roles, text preprocessing is essential for improving the accuracy of machine learning models.
what_you_will_learn: Tokenization — splitting text into individual words or tokens using libraries like NLTK, spaCy, or gensim
Stopword removal — removing common words like 'the', 'and', etc. that do not add much value to the analysis
Stemming and Lemmatization — reducing words to their base form (e.g., 'running' becomes 'run') using techniques like Porter Stemmer or WordNet Lemmatizer
Remove special characters and punctuation — cleaning text by removing non-alphanumeric characters
Handling missing values — dealing with missing data in text datasets, such as replacing with a specific value or imputing with a suitable replacement strategy
Text normalization — converting all text to lowercase, removing extra whitespace, etc.
Named Entity Recognition (NER) — identifying and categorizing named entities like people, places, organizations using tools like spaCy or Stanford CoreNLP
Part-of-speech tagging — identifying the grammatical category of each word in a sentence (e.g., noun, verb, adjective)
Dependency parsing — analyzing the grammatical structure of sentences to identify relationships between words

knowledge.what_it_is: Word embeddings are a fundamental concept in natural language processing (NLP) that represent words as dense vectors in a high-dimensional space. This allows for efficient computation of semantic relationships between words. Word2Vec and GloVe are two popular algorithms for learning word embeddings.
what_you_will_learn: Vector space model — representing words as fixed-length vectors to capture semantic meaning
Word2Vec algorithm — training word embeddings using continuous bag-of-words (CBOW) or skip-gram models
GloVe algorithm — learning word embeddings by factorizing a matrix of co-occurrence counts
Word embedding dimensions — choosing optimal dimensionality for word vectors (e.g., 100, 300)
Context window size — selecting an appropriate context window size for Word2Vec training
Subsampling frequent words — reducing the impact of high-frequency words on word embeddings
Negative sampling — using noise contrastive estimation to improve word embedding quality
Word vector normalization — techniques for normalizing word vectors (e.g., L1, L2 norm)
Word similarity metrics — computing semantic similarity between word vectors (e.g., cosine similarity)
Word analogies — using word embeddings to solve analogy tasks (e.g., king : man :: woman : ?)
Word clustering — grouping similar words together based on their word embeddings
Word2Vec pre-trained models — leveraging pre-trained Word2Vec models for downstream NLP tasks
GloVe pre-trained models — using pre-trained GloVe models for efficient NLP applications

knowledge.what_it_is: Transformers architecture is a type of neural network designed to handle sequential data, such as natural language processing tasks like text classification, machine translation, and question answering. It was first introduced by Vaswani et al. in their paper 'Attention Is All You Need' in 2017 and has since become a widely used architecture for NLP tasks.
what_you_will_learn: Self-attention mechanism — the core component of Transformers that allows the model to weigh the importance of different input elements
Encoder-decoder structure — the typical architecture of Transformers, consisting of an encoder that processes the input sequence and a decoder that generates the output sequence
Positional encoding — a technique used to incorporate positional information into the input sequence without increasing the dimensionality of the input
Multi-head attention — a variant of self-attention that allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions
Layer normalization — a technique used to normalize the activations of each layer in the network
Residual connections — a technique used to facilitate the training of deep networks by allowing the gradients to flow through the network more easily
Feed-forward neural network (FFNN) layers — fully connected feed-forward neural networks that are used as building blocks for the encoder and decoder
Gated recurrent unit (GRU) or long short-term memory (LSTM) layers — variants of recurrent neural networks that can be used in place of FFNN layers
Pre-training and fine-tuning — a common practice in NLP where a pre-trained model is fine-tuned on a specific task to adapt it to the new task
Masked language modeling — a technique used during pre-training where some input tokens are randomly masked and the model has to predict these tokens
Next sentence prediction — a technique used during pre-training where two sentences are given as input and the model has to predict whether they are adjacent in the original text
BERT, RoBERTa, and other variants — popular pre-trained models that can be fine-tuned for specific NLP tasks
Hugging Face Transformers library — a widely used library for implementing and training Transformers architectures in Python
PyTorch or TensorFlow implementation — understanding how to implement the Transformer architecture using these popular deep learning frameworks

knowledge.what_it_is: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) is a pre-trained language model developed by Google, based on the BERT architecture. It has been fine-tuned for various NLP tasks such as question answering, sentiment analysis, and text classification. BERT variants include RoBERTa, ALBERT, DistilBERT, and others.
what_you_will_learn: BERT architecture — multi-layer bidirectional transformer encoder with self-attention mechanism
Masked Language Modeling (MLM) task — predicting randomly masked tokens in the input sequence
Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) task — predicting whether two sentences are adjacent in the original text
Pre-training and fine-tuning — adapting pre-trained models to specific NLP tasks with minimal training data
RoBERTa architecture — a variant of BERT with a different tokenization scheme and training objectives
ALBERT architecture — a variant of BERT with factorized embedding parameterization and cross-layer parameter sharing
DistilBERT architecture — a smaller, more efficient version of BERT with knowledge distillation
BERT variants for specific NLP tasks — such as question answering (SQuAD), sentiment analysis (IMDB), and text classification (GLUE)
Fine-tuning pre-trained models using transfer learning — adapting pre-trained weights to new tasks with minimal training data
Using BERT in production environments — integrating pre-trained models into pipelines for real-world applications
BERT-based sentence embeddings — generating fixed-length vector representations of sentences using BERT
BERT-based document embeddings — generating fixed-length vector representations of documents using BERT
BERT-based topic modeling — identifying topics and themes in large datasets using BERT
Using Hugging Face Transformers library for BERT implementation — leveraging a popular, widely-used library for NLP tasks

knowledge.what_it_is: GPT models are a type of transformer-based language model that can generate coherent and context-specific text, developed by OpenAI. They are trained on massive datasets to predict the next token in a sequence, enabling applications such as conversational AI, text summarization, and content generation.
what_you_will_learn: Transformer architecture — understanding how GPT models use self-attention mechanisms for parallel processing
Masked language modeling — training GPT models on masked input to predict missing tokens
Next token prediction — the core objective of GPT models, predicting the next token in a sequence
Autoregressive decoding — generating text one token at a time using the predicted probabilities
GPT-2 architecture — understanding the 12-layer transformer model with 128M parameters used in early GPT research
GPT-3 architecture — understanding the 175B parameter model with improved performance and capabilities
Adversarial training — techniques to improve robustness of GPT models against adversarial attacks
Knowledge distillation — transferring knowledge from large teacher models to smaller student models
Few-shot learning — enabling GPT models to learn from a few examples and generalize to new tasks
Conversational flow — understanding how to design conversational interfaces using GPT models
Text summarization — generating summaries of long documents using GPT models
Content generation — creating coherent and engaging content using GPT models
Emotion detection — recognizing emotions in text generated by GPT models
Sentiment analysis — understanding the sentiment behind text generated by GPT models

knowledge.what_it_is: Named Entity Recognition (NER) is a subtask of Natural Language Processing (NLP) that involves identifying and categorizing named entities in unstructured text into predefined categories such as person, organization, location, date, time, etc. It is a crucial component of many NLP applications including information extraction, sentiment analysis, and question answering.
what_you_will_learn: Tokenization — splitting text into individual words or tokens for NER processing
Part-of-speech (POS) tagging — identifying the grammatical category of each word (noun, verb, adjective, etc.) to disambiguate entity types
Named Entity Recognition models — understanding the different architectures and techniques used in NER including rule-based, machine learning, and deep learning approaches
CRF (Conditional Random Field) — a popular machine learning algorithm for sequence labeling tasks like NER
BiLSTM (Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory) — a type of recurrent neural network (RNN) architecture used in many modern NER models
BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) — pre-trained language model fine-tuned for NER tasks with state-of-the-art performance
spaCy — popular Python library for NLP and NER, providing high-performance, streamlined processing of text data
Stanford CoreNLP — Java library for NLP and NER, offering a wide range of tools and resources for text analysis
NLTK (Natural Language Toolkit) — Python library for NLP and NER, providing access to corpora, tokenizers, taggers, parsers, and other NLP tools

knowledge.what_it_is: Sentiment analysis is a subfield of natural language processing (NLP) that involves automatically determining the sentiment or emotional tone behind a piece of text, such as positive, negative, or neutral. It's widely used in customer service, market research, and social media monitoring to gain insights into public opinion.
what_you_will_learn: Text preprocessing techniques for sentiment analysis — tokenization, stemming, lemmatization, stopword removal
Sentiment lexicons and dictionaries — SentiWordNet, VADER, AFINN-111
Machine learning algorithms for sentiment classification — Naive Bayes, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Random Forest
Deep learning architectures for sentiment analysis — Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)
Word embeddings and their impact on sentiment analysis — Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT
Handling out-of-vocabulary words and unknown entities in sentiment analysis — named entity recognition (NER) and part-of-speech (POS) tagging
Sentiment analysis with Transformers — BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT
Multitask learning for sentiment analysis — combining multiple tasks such as sentiment classification and aspect-based sentiment analysis
Handling imbalanced datasets in sentiment analysis — oversampling minority class, undersampling majority class, SMOTE
Evaluating sentiment analysis models — metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC

knowledge.what_it_is: Hugging Face Transformers is an open-source library of pre-trained models for natural language processing (NLP) tasks, developed by Hugging Face. It provides a simple interface to fine-tune these models on specific tasks and datasets, enabling data scientists to build custom NLP models without extensive expertise in deep learning.
what_you_will_learn: Transformers architecture — understanding the encoder-decoder structure and self-attention mechanism
Pre-trained models — accessing pre-trained models for various NLP tasks such as sentiment analysis, question answering, and text classification
Model fine-tuning — adapting pre-trained models to specific tasks using transfer learning and customizing model weights
Tokenizer — understanding the role of tokenizers in processing input data and converting it into a format suitable for models
Dataset loading — loading datasets from various sources such as CSV, JSON, and Hugging Face's Datasets library
Training loop — implementing training loops using Hugging Face's Trainer class and customizing hyperparameters
Evaluation metrics — understanding evaluation metrics such as accuracy, F1 score, and ROUGE score for NLP tasks
Model saving and loading — saving and loading pre-trained models to/from disk or model hubs
Model ensemble — combining multiple models using techniques like stacking and bagging
Custom dataset creation — creating custom datasets from scratch using Hugging Face's Datasets library
Hugging Face Hub — publishing and sharing models on the Hugging Face Hub for collaboration and reuse
Transformers variants — understanding different variants of Transformers such as BERT, RoBERTa, and ALBERT
Attention mechanisms — understanding attention mechanisms such as self-attention, multi-head attention, and relative position encoding
Positional embeddings — understanding positional embeddings and their role in handling sequential data

knowledge.what_it_is: The Hadoop ecosystem is an open-source, distributed computing framework that enables scalable data processing and storage. It was created by Doug Cutting and Mike Cafarella and is now maintained by the Apache Software Foundation.
what_you_will_learn: HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) — a distributed file system for storing large datasets
YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) — a resource management layer for scheduling jobs on Hadoop clusters
MapReduce — a programming model for processing data in parallel across a cluster of nodes
Pig — a high-level data processing language that abstracts away the complexity of MapReduce
Hive — a data warehousing and SQL-like query language for Hadoop
Sqoop — a tool for transferring data between Hadoop and relational databases
Flume — a distributed, reliable system for efficiently collecting, aggregating, and moving large amounts of log data
Kafka — a distributed streaming platform for high-throughput, fault-tolerant data processing
HBase — a NoSQL, column-family based database that sits on top of HDFS
HiveQL — a SQL-like query language for querying and managing data in Hive
Impala — an interactive SQL engine for Hadoop that allows for real-time analytics
Tez — a general-purpose distributed processing framework for Hadoop
Oozie — a workflow scheduler system for managing complex data processing pipelines
Falcon — a unified, scalable platform for data warehousing and ETL (Extract, Transform, Load)
Ambari — an open-source management tool for provisioning, managing, and monitoring Hadoop clusters

knowledge.what_it_is: Apache Spark is an open-source, distributed computing engine that provides high-level APIs in Java, Python, Scala, and R to process large-scale data sets. It's widely used for big data processing, machine learning, and data engineering tasks.
what_you_will_learn: Distributed Data Structures — Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs) and DataFrames/Datasets
Spark Core APIs — SparkContext, SparkSession, and RDD operations (map, filter, reduce)
Data Serialization — using Kryo, Java serialization, or custom serializers
Caching and Persistence — using Spark's caching mechanism and storing data in memory or disk
Job Scheduling — understanding Spark's job scheduling mechanisms and optimizing task execution
Data Partitioning — understanding how Spark partitions data for efficient processing
Shuffle Operations — understanding the shuffle process and its impact on performance
Broadcast Variables — using broadcast variables to share large datasets across tasks
Accumulators — using accumulators to compute aggregate values in parallel
Spark SQL — creating and querying structured data with Spark's SQL engine
DataFrames/Datasets API — working with DataFrames and Datasets for efficient data processing
DataFrame Operations — performing common operations like filtering, sorting, and aggregating on DataFrames
Joining DataFrames — understanding how to join multiple DataFrames efficiently
GroupBy and Aggregation — using GroupBy and aggregation functions to analyze data
Window Functions — applying window functions for advanced analytics tasks
Machine Learning with MLlib — using Spark's MLlib library for machine learning tasks
Model Training and Evaluation — understanding how to train and evaluate machine learning models in Spark
Hyperparameter Tuning — using techniques like grid search, random search, or Bayesian optimization to tune hyperparameters

knowledge.what_it_is: Distributed computing concepts enable efficient processing of large datasets by breaking them down into smaller, manageable chunks that can be processed concurrently across multiple nodes or machines. This approach is crucial for AI and data science applications where massive amounts of data need to be analyzed in a reasonable timeframe.
what_you_will_learn: MapReduce — a programming model used to process large datasets by dividing them into smaller pieces, processing each piece in parallel, and combining the results
Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) — a scalable, fault-tolerant storage system for storing and managing large amounts of data across multiple nodes
Apache Spark — an open-source engine for processing large-scale data sets, providing high-level APIs in Java, Python, Scala, and R
Distributed databases — architectures that allow data to be split across multiple machines or nodes, such as Google's Bigtable or Amazon's DynamoDB
Cloud computing platforms — infrastructure-as-a-service (IaaS) providers like AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud that offer scalable, on-demand computing resources for distributed processing
Message Passing Interface (MPI) — a standardized API for parallel programming in distributed environments, used for tasks like scientific simulations and data analysis
Distributed machine learning frameworks — libraries like TensorFlow Distributed, PyTorch Distributed, or Horovod that enable training large models across multiple machines
Data sharding — dividing a dataset into smaller chunks (shards) to distribute them across multiple nodes or machines for parallel processing
Load balancing — techniques for distributing workload evenly across multiple nodes or machines to prevent bottlenecks and ensure efficient processing
Fault tolerance — mechanisms for handling node failures in distributed systems, such as replication or checkpointing
Scalability — designing distributed systems to handle increasing workloads by adding more nodes or resources as needed
Consistency models — trade-offs between consistency and availability in distributed databases, including strong consistency, eventual consistency, and causal consistency
CAP theorem — a fundamental concept in distributed systems that states it's impossible for a system to simultaneously guarantee all three of consistency, availability, and partition tolerance
Paxos algorithm — a consensus protocol used in distributed systems to ensure agreement on values across multiple nodes or machines

knowledge.what_it_is: MapReduce is a programming model for processing large data sets in parallel across a cluster of nodes, developed by Google and now maintained by Apache. It's widely used in big data analytics, machine learning, and data science applications.
what_you_will_learn: Map function — transforming input data into key-value pairs using user-defined functions
Reduce function — aggregating the output from map tasks to produce a final result, often using aggregation operators like sum or average
Mapper configuration — defining the number of mappers, mapper memory, and other resources for efficient processing
Reducer configuration — specifying the number of reducers, reducer memory, and other settings for optimal performance
Shuffle and sort — understanding how map output is shuffled and sorted before being fed to reduce tasks
Combiner function — optimizing intermediate results by combining nearby map outputs before sending them to reducers
Partitioning — dividing input data into smaller chunks for efficient processing using techniques like hash partitioning or range partitioning
TaskTracker — managing the execution of mapper and reducer tasks on individual nodes in a cluster
JobTracker — overseeing the entire MapReduce job, including task assignment, progress tracking, and error handling
Input Splits — dividing large input files into smaller chunks for efficient processing by multiple mappers
Output Formats — understanding how to write output data from reducers in various formats like text, sequence file, or Avro
MapReduce programming model — designing MapReduce jobs using APIs like Hadoop Java API or Apache Spark
YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) — managing resources and scheduling tasks on a cluster using YARN's resource manager
HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) — storing and retrieving data in a distributed file system for efficient processing
